summary: Gruber shares an updated version of his popular 'Grab HTML' AppleScript for BBEdit, which fetches web page source code via the command-line curl utility. The script is a Mac OS X replacement for Dan Crevier's 1996 HTML Grabber plug-in that only worked in Mac OS 9. It demonstrates how AppleScript's do shell script command bridges the Mac GUI and Unix command line, offering a dialog box to enter a URL and optionally include HTTP headers. Gruber walks through the implementation details, explaining how BBEdit properties like auto-language detection and window naming enhance the workflow.
key_points: The script replaces a 1996 BBEdit plug-in by combining AppleScript's GUI capabilities with the Unix curl command
Mac OS X's do shell script command puts the entire Unix command-line toolset at the scripter's fingertips, making it an easy-to-use programming interface
Setting window contents as a property during creation triggers BBEdit's automatic language detection and syntax coloring
The script remembers the last URL entered between invocations by using an AppleScript property
money_quotes: This sort of AppleScript/shell hybrid shows Mac OS X at its best.
While a command line by itself does not make for a good user interface, it can be an amazingly easy-to-use programming interface.
The do shell script command puts the whole Unix command-line toolset at your fingertips.
Sort of like the View Source command in a web browser, except much more convenient if you're already working in BBEdit.
I'm aware that "With Headers" is an awkward name for a button because it isn't a verb; but the display dialog command insists on making all three buttons the same width.
key_insight: Mac OS X's ability to bridge AppleScript's GUI dialog boxes with Unix command-line tools like curl exemplifies the platform's unique strength as both a user-friendly and programmer-friendly environment.
